elements.sent-2411-001: Watermarking has offered an effective approach to distinguishing text generated by large language models (LLMs) from human-written text.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, the pervasive presence of human edits on LLM-generated text dilutes watermark signals, thereby significantly degrading detection performance of existing methods.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this paper, by modeling human edits through mixture model detection, we introduce a new method in the form of a truncated goodness-of-fit test for detecting watermarked text under human edits, which we refer to as \Algo.
elements.sent-2411-004: We prove that the \Algo~test achieves optimality in robust detection of the Gumbel-max watermark in a certain asymptotic regime of substantial text modifications and vanishing watermark signals.
elements.sent-2411-005: Importantly, \Algo~achieves this optimality adaptively as it does not require precise knowledge of human edit levels or probabilistic specifications of the LLMs, in contrast to the optimal but impractical (Neyman--Pearson) likelihood ratio test.
elements.sent-2411-006: Moreover, we establish that the \Algo~test attains the highest detection efficiency rate in a certain regime of moderate text modifications.
elements.sent-2411-007: In stark contrast, we show that sum-based detection rules, as employed by existing methods, fail to achieve optimal robustness in both regimes because the additive nature of their statistics is less resilient to edit-induced noise.
elements.sent-2411-008: Finally, we demonstrate the competitive and sometimes superior empirical performance of the \Algo~test on both synthetic data and open-source LLMs in the OPT and LLaMA families.
elements.sent-2411-009: Large language models (LLMs) have recently emerged as a transformative technique for generating human-like text and other media~\citep{touvron2023llama,openai2023,achiam2023gpt .}
elements.sent-2411-010: While this advancement boosts productivity across various industries, it also introduces risks related to the ownership and creation of content.
elements.sent-2411-011: These risks include the spread of misinformation \citep{zellers2019defending,weidinger2021ethical,starbird2019disinformation , challenges to education and academic integrity \citep{stokel2022ai,milano2023large , and issues concerning data authenticity \citep{radford2023robust,shumailov2023curse,das2024under .}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: These problems highlight the urgent need for methodologies to authenticate and verify the origin of text, specifically, determining whether it is generated by LLMs or humans.
elements.sent-2411-013: Watermarking text during generation by LLMs has offered a principled and viable approach to resolving these issues \citep{kirchenbauer2023watermark,scott2023watermarking,christ2023undetectable .}
elements.sent-2411-014: Since 2023, a variety of watermarking schemes have been introduced \citep{fernandez2023three,kuditipudi2023robust,hu2023unbiased,wu2023dipmark,zhao2024permute,zhao2024provable,liu2024adaptive,giboulot2024watermax,fu2024gumbelsoft,Dathathri2024,xie2024debiasing .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Loosely speaking, a watermarking scheme embeds signals into the process of generating text by using controlled pseudorandomness.
elements.sent-2411-016: This is made possible by the probabilistic nature of LLMs through next-token prediction (NTP) for sequentially generating text.
elements.sent-2411-017: The watermark signals are nearly unnoticeable, if possible, to human readers but are provably detectable once a verifier has knowledge of how the pseudorandomness is constructed \citep{christ2023undetectable .}
elements.figure-2411-001: \begin{flushright} \begin{userbox} \begin{minipage}{\textwidth} Prompt: How can statistics benefit the study of watermarks for LLMs? \end{minipage} \end{userbox} \end{flushright} \begin{flushleft} \begin{gptbox} \begin{minipage}{0.05\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=6mm]{figs/logo.pdf} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{0.94\textwidth} ChatGPT: Statistics serves as an invaluable conduit for discerning the intricate interplay of resilience, detectability, and adaptive robustness in watermarking frameworks tailored for large language models, thereby anchoring a profound understanding of their operational fidelity across diverse linguistic landscapes. \end{minipage} \end{gptbox} \end{flushleft} \begin{flushright} \begin{userbox} \begin{minipage}{\textwidth} Human edits: Statistics serves as \textcolor{orange}{a valuable tool} for \textcolor{orange}{examining} the \sout{intricate} interplay of resilience, detectability, and \sout{adaptive} robustness \sout{in} \textcolor{blue!60!black}{of} watermarking frameworks \sout{tailored} for large language models, \textcolor{orange}{helping} \sout{anchoring} \textcolor{blue!60!black}{to build} a \textcolor{orange}{clearer} understanding of their \textcolor{orange}{reliability} across \textcolor{orange}{different} linguistic \textcolor{orange}{contexts}. \end{minipage} \end{userbox} \end{flushright} \caption{How users modify ChatGPT's response through edits such as substitution, deletion, and insertion. \textcolor{orange}{Orange underline} marks substitutions, \sout{black strikethrough} indicates deletions, and \textcolor{blue!60!black}{blue underline} highlights insertions. } \label{fig:human-edit}
elements.sent-2411-018: In real-world scenarios, however, text generated from LLMs often undergoes various forms of human edits before its use (see Figure \ref{fig:human-edit for an illustration), which presents perhaps the most significant challenge for applying watermarks \citep{kirchenbauer2023reliability,nature_editorial .}}
elements.sent-2411-019: In the simplest case, a user of LLMs might replace several words of the generated text, either to improve the text from the user's perspective or make it less LLM-sounding.
elements.sent-2411-020: This simple editing process would weaken the watermark since the signals from the modified tokens---the smallest unit in text generation, which can be a word or a sub-word---turn to noise.
elements.sent-2411-021: Worse, how the editing process proceeds is unknown to the verifier.
elements.sent-2411-022: For example, it is not clear if a token in the text is generated by the LLM or has been modified by a human.
elements.sent-2411-023: In light of this, a watermark detection rule should not only be efficient in the edit-free scenario but also, perhaps more importantly, its detection power should not be unduly affected by various forms of human edits.
elements.sent-2411-024: Equally important is adaptivity: the rule should perform well without prior knowledge of how or to what extent the text has been edited, ensuring practical applicability in handling diverse and unpredictable editing behaviors.
elements.sent-2411-025: To get a handle on how robust and adaptive existing watermark detection rules are against human edits, we conduct a numerical experiment and present the results in Figure~\ref{fig:intro .}
elements.sent-2411-026: The experiment is concerned with the Gumbel-max watermark \citep{scott2023watermarking , which has been implemented internally at OpenAI and is the first unbiased\footnote{A watermarking scheme is (approximately) unbiased if the watermarked LLM generates each token with (approximately) the same probability distribution as the unwatermarked counterpart.. watermark.}}
elements.sent-2411-027: With 5\% of the tokens modified by humans, the detection power when text length is 400 drops from 87.8\% in the edit-free case to 64.7\% with paraphrase edits and further to 30.2\% with adversarial edits.
elements.sent-2411-028: While this significant performance degradation is concerning, it is not surprising as these detection rules \citep{li2024statistical,scott2023watermarking are developed without any robustness consideration, in particular, assuming there is no signal corruption due to human edits.}
elements.sent-2411-029: More critically, the editing process would render the computation of pseudorandomness, which takes as input the before-edited tokens \citep{kirchenbauer2023watermark,scott2023watermarking , impossible for detecting watermarks.}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.58\textwidth]{figs/intro-0.3.pdf} \caption{ Empirical study of detection methods for the Gumbel-max watermark. Statistical power is evaluated at a 0.01 significance level using 1,000 prompts from the C4 dataset~\citep{raffel2020exploring} and 400 generated tokens per prompt with the OPT-1.3B model~\citep{zhang2022opt} (temperature 0.3). Paraphrase edits randomly replace 5\% of tokens with WordNet synonyms~\citep{miller1995wordnet}, while adversarial edits replace the 5\% with the strongest watermark signals. Thinner curves indicate higher levels of human edits. See Appendix \ref{exp:detials} for details. } \label{fig:intro}
elements.sent-2411-030: Thus, there is a pressing need to develop robust and adaptive methods for detecting watermarks in LLM-generated text that may undergo human edits.
elements.sent-2411-031: Existing work on watermark detection typically assumes a scenario where each token contributes a signal to the watermark.
elements.sent-2411-032: For example, \citet{li2024statistical introduced a statistical framework for evaluating detection efficiency based on sum-based statistics that measure the cumulative watermark strength across all tokens.}
elements.sent-2411-033: However, this framework operates under the ideal assumption that tokens are either fully human-written or entirely generated by an LLM.
elements.sent-2411-034: When applied to human-edited text---a combination of human- and LLM-generated tokens---sum-based statistics may be easily corrupted by noise, leading to substantial degradation in efficiency, as illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig:intro .}
elements.sent-2411-035: In this paper, we address the robustness of detecting LLM watermarks from a statistical viewpoint, providing both a rigorous framework and methodologies with theoretical guarantees alongside empirical validation.
elements.sent-2411-036: A primary challenge lies in formally modeling how humans edit LLM-generated text---a necessary step for analyzing the efficiency of robust watermark detection.
elements.sent-2411-037: Recognizing the complexity of human editing processes, we consider three editing operations: substitution, insertion, and deletion, which humans apply sequentially while reviewing LLM-generated text.
elements.sent-2411-038: While the specifics of the editing process cannot be inferred from the modified text alone, a crucial observation we make is that, due to the indistinguishability between pseudorandomness and true randomness, each token in the text under scrutiny contributes either noise or signal, depending on whether it has been affected by human edits.
elements.sent-2411-039: Moreover, the noise follows a fixed and known distribution under the framework of \citet{li2024statistical , while the signal distribution depends on the probability distribution of the token predicted by the LLM.}
elements.sent-2411-040: We call this multinomial distribution the NTP distribution and denote it by $\bP_t = (P_{t,\token )_{\token \in \Voca}$ for predicting the $t$-th token, where $\Voca$ denotes the token vocabulary.}
elements.sent-2411-041: For example, the vocabulary size $|\Voca|$ is 50,257 for GPT-2/3.5 series models \citep{radford2019language,brown2020language and 32,000 for the LLaMA-2 series models \citep{touvron2023llama .}}
elements.sent-2411-042: Under this human editing model, we introduce a binary process $\{\eta_t\}_{t=1 ^n$ to indicate whether the watermark signal remains at the position of the $t$-th token in a text of length $n$.}
elements.sent-2411-043: This allows us to model the watermark signal across all tokens as a mixture distribution, with one component being a signal distribution when $\eta_t = 1$ and the other component being the noise distribution when $\eta_t = 0$.
elements.sent-2411-044: First and foremost, a key question is when it is statistically possible to distinguish this mixture as the alternative hypothesis (indicating watermarked text under human editing) from the pure noise distribution as the null hypothesis (indicating human-written text), for example, in the case of the Gumbel-max watermark.
elements.sent-2411-045: This is addressed in the following finding of our paper (we write $a_n \asymp b_n$ if there exist constants $C_1$ and $C_2$ such that $C_1 a_n \le b_n \le C_2 a_n$ for all $n$):.
elements.sent-2411-046: We consider an asymptotic regime where the text length $n$ tends to infinity, but individual tokens contain less watermark signal.
elements.sent-2411-047: Specifically, we assume $\sP(\eta_t = 1) = \EB[\eta_t] \asymp n^{-p}$ and $1 - \max_{\token \in \Voca P_{t,\token \asymp n^{-q}$ for constants $0 < p, q < 1$, inspired by the sparse mixture detection problem \cite{donoho2004higher .}}}
elements.sent-2411-048: Detection becomes more challenging as either $p$ or $q$ increases.
elements.sent-2411-049: A larger value of $p$ means fewer tokens contribute to the watermark after human editing, resulting in a diluted watermark; similarly, a larger value of $q$ indicates that the NTP distributions are less regular due to being approximately degenerate, which weakens watermark signals (see elaboration in Section~\ref{sec:detectability ).}
elements.sent-2411-050: Our finding is that the optimal detection region in the $(p, q)$ plane has boundary $q + 2p = 1$.
elements.sent-2411-051: Explicitly, when $q + 2p > 1$, no test can asymptotically achieve zero Type I and Type II errors for the Gumbel-max watermark; when $q + 2p < 1$, in stark contrast, both Type I and Type II errors approach zero using the likelihood-ratio test.
elements.sent-2411-052: While the likelihood-ratio test achieves theoretical optimality, it requires knowledge of the watermark fraction $\EB[\eta_t]$ and all NTP distributions $\bP_t$ for $t = 1, \ldots, n$.
elements.sent-2411-053: To make this boundary practically relevant, it is essential to develop a practical method that can reliably separate the null and alternative distributions as $n \rightarrow \infty$.
elements.sent-2411-054: For this purpose, it is instructive to examine the statistical limits of sum-based test statistics as employed in \cite{li2024statistical,kuditipudi2023robust,fernandez2023three .}
elements.sent-2411-055: We prove that these sum-based detection rules fail to achieve the optimal boundary $q + 2p = 1$ in general, and more precisely, the best possible detection boundary these methods can achieve is $q + p = 1/2$.
elements.sent-2411-056: This analysis offers insights into the causes of this suboptimality, which we leverage to develop a new, practically adaptive method that achieves the optimal boundary:.
elements.sent-2411-057: Our approach utilizes the empirical cumulative distribution function of p-values from the text under detection, comparing it with the null counterpart through a form of divergence \citep{jager2007goodness .}
elements.sent-2411-058: Unlike sum-based approaches \cite{li2024statistical,kuditipudi2023robust,fernandez2023three , this goodness-of-fit test adaptively identifies areas of significant departure between null and alternative distributions without requiring prior knowledge of the watermark fraction or NTP regularity.}
elements.sent-2411-059: Moreover, a crucial innovation is to truncate the search for the location to filter out extreme scores for stability.
elements.sent-2411-060: This gives a family of truncated goodness-of-fit tests, which we refer to as \Algo~for short (details in Algorithm \ref{alg:detection ).}
elements.sent-2411-061: We show that \Algo~achieves the robust detection boundary $q + 2p = 1$ without relying on any tuning parameters, thereby possessing adaptive optimality \cite{donoho2004higher,donoho2015higher .}
elements.sent-2411-062: Figure \ref{fig:intro-better-result demonstrates the robustness of \Algo~in detecting the Gumbel-max watermark in human-edited text.}

elements.sent-2411-063: Having established the optimality of \Algo~for sparse watermark detection, we further examine its performance in a regime of ``dense'' watermark signals.
elements.sent-2411-064: More precisely, we consider the setting where the watermark fraction $\EB[\eta_t] \equiv \eps$ remains constant, and the regularity level of the NTP distribution satisfies $1 - \max_{\token \in \Voca P_{t,\token \ge \Delta$ for some constant $0 < \Delta < 1$.}}
elements.sent-2411-065: Below is our third main result in this paper:.
elements.sent-2411-066: We show that the \Algo~test achieves the highest class-dependent efficiency under the framework of watermarks for LLMs proposed by \citet{li2024statistical .}
elements.sent-2411-067: This optimality is adaptive in that it does not rely on any prior knowledge of $\Delta$ or $\eps$, whereas existing sum-based detection rules fail to achieve the same level of efficiency due to their lack of adaptivity for dense watermark detection (see Figure~\ref{fig:efficiency for illustration).}
elements.sent-2411-068: This finding is unexpected as the \Algo~test was originally motivated by sparse watermark detection.
elements.sent-2411-069: Taken together, our results show that the \Algo~test achieves optimality in both sparse and dense regimes of watermark detection.
elements.sent-2411-070: Most robust watermarking methods focus on algorithmic or cryptographic approaches.
elements.sent-2411-071: Algorithmic methods emphasize resilient watermarking designs \citep{yoo2023robust, kuditipudi2023robust, zhu2024duwak, hou2024semstamp, ren2023robust, giboulot2024watermax , while cryptographic approaches protect watermark signals through robust pseudorandom codes \citep{golowich2024edit, christ2024pseudorandom .}}
elements.sent-2411-072: However, rigorous statistical analysis of robustness in watermark detection remains limited, largely due to the absence of a cohesive statistical framework.
elements.sent-2411-073: Definitions of robustness also vary widely across studies.
elements.sent-2411-074: Our approach introduces a statistical perspective by modeling human edits as a mixture distribution, enabling the establishment of detection boundaries and class-dependent efficiency rates.
elements.sent-2411-075: Another statistical approach to robustness is the edit tolerance limit, which quantifies the maximum proportion of edits a detection method can withstand while remaining effective.
elements.sent-2411-076: Studies often apply this perspective to the green-red list watermark, initially proposed by \citet{kirchenbauer2023watermark, kirchenbauer2023reliability and valued for its simplicity \citep{fernandez2023three, hu2023unbiased, wu2023dipmark, zhu2024duwak, liu2024a .}}
elements.sent-2411-077: Additional refinements of this approach are discussed in \citep{kuditipudi2023robust, cai2024towards, huang2023towards .}
elements.sent-2411-078: Other forms of robustness include decoder-focused and cryptographic approaches.
elements.sent-2411-079: In cryptographic studies, robustness is designed to make pseudorandom codes resistant to a fixed proportion of human edits \citep{golowich2024edit, christ2024pseudorandom .}
elements.sent-2411-080: Our statistical viewpoint complements these approaches, providing an orthogonal framework for assessing robustness.
elements.sent-2411-081: Robust statistics, which traditionally addresses resilience to outliers and perturbations, provides a relevant foundation for our study \citep{huber1992robust, huber2011robust, diakonikolas2023algorithmic .}
elements.sent-2411-082: Our mixture formulation connects with Huber’s contamination model \citep{huber1992robust ; however, unlike in Huber’s model where the contaminated distribution is typically unknown, in watermark detection, this distribution is known.}
elements.sent-2411-083: Additionally, our work is related to sparse mixture detection, first introduced by \citet{dobrusin1958statistical to identify sparse signals, with further developments in mixtures model \citep{ingster1996some,donoho2004higher,tony2011optimal,cai2014optimal,arias2017distribution and rare-signal models \citep{jin2016rare,ke2014covariance,jin2021optimal .}}}
elements.sent-2411-084: Robust watermark detection also diverges from traditional sparse detection due to the autoregressive nature of text generation, which results in time-varying signal (or NTP) distributions.
elements.sent-2411-085: While some methods address inhomogeneity using stationary Gaussian processes \citep{hall2008properties or temporal correlations \citep{hall2010innovated , they are inapplicable to text generation.}}
elements.sent-2411-086: Our theoretical analysis directly leverages the autoregressive nature to analyze inhomogeneity in watermark detection, as detailed in Section \ref{sec:theory .}

elements.sent-2411-093: LLMs, like GPT models \citep{radford2019language,brown2020language , generate text by sampling tokens autoregressively.}
elements.sent-2411-094: Given an existing sequence $\token_{1:(t-1) := \token_1 \token_2 \cdots \token_{t-1}$, the LLM computes the NTP distribution $\bP_t := (P_{t,w )_{w \in \Voca}$ and samples the next token $\token_t$.}}
elements.sent-2411-095: The NTP distribution $\bP_t$ is unknown to the verifier since it depends on all prior generated tokens, the user-supplied prompt, as well as system prompts that are hidden from users \citep{vaswani2017attention .}
elements.sent-2411-096: We denote this dependence explicitly as $\bP_t = \MM(\token_{1:(t-1) )$.}
elements.sent-2411-097: A watermarked LLM embeds watermarks during next-token sampling by generating a pseudorandom variable $\xi_t$ via $\xi_t = \AM(\token_{(t-m):(t-1) , \Key)$ where $\AM$ is a (deterministic) hash function, $m$ is the context window, and $\Key$ is a secret key.}
elements.sent-2411-098: The next token $\token_t$ is then computed as: $\token_t := \SM(\bP_t, \xi_t)$, where $\SM$ is a deterministic decoder function.
elements.sent-2411-099: The pseudorandom variables $\xi_{1:n}$ can be recovered with the sequence $\token_{1:n}$, $\AM$, and $\Key$.
elements.sent-2411-100: Without the key, $\xi_t$ behaves like a random variable due to the cryptographic properties of hash functions \citep{barak2021book, schneier1996applied .}
elements.sent-2411-102: The decoder $\SM$ is unbiased if it follows the NTP distribution $\bP$, meaning $\PB_{\xi \sim \pi (\SM(\bP, \xi) = \token) = P_{\token}$ for any $\bP$ and token $\token$.}
elements.sent-2411-103: An unbiased decoder corresponds to standard multinomial sampling methods.

elements.sent-2411-104: In this framework, determining whether a watermark is embedded is reduced to testing for the statistical dependence between the text $\token_{1:n}$ and the corresponding pseudorandom numbers $\xi_{1:n}$.
elements.sent-2411-105: The two hypotheses are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-106: The null hypothesis $H_0$ asserts that no watermark is present and that $\token_{1:n}$ is human-written.
elements.sent-2411-107: Since the NTP distributions of human writing are generally unknown, a pivotal statistic $\Yars_t = Y(\token_t, \xi_t)$ is introduced.
elements.sent-2411-108: The pivotal function $Y(\cdot, \cdot)$ ensures that $\Yars_t$ has a known distribution under $H_0$, as long as $\token_t$ is independent of $\xi_t$, regardless of $\token_t$'s distribution.
elements.sent-2411-109: Working Hypothesis 2.2 in \citet{li2024statistical posits that human writing should always be treated as independent of watermarked generation due to the undisclosed watermarking process.}
elements.sent-2411-111: The alternative hypothesis $H_1$ asserts that $\token_{1:n}$ is generated by a watermarked LLM.
elements.sent-2411-112: The watermarking process introduces a dependence between $\token_t$ and $\xi_t$ through the decoder: $\token_t = \SM(\bP_t, \xi_t)$.
elements.sent-2411-113: As a result, under $H_1$, the distribution of $Y_t$ for a given $\bP_t$ differs from its null distribution $\mu_0$.
elements.sent-2411-114: Working Hypothesis 2.1 in \citep{li2024statistical assumes that $\xi_{1:n}$ are i.i.d.}
elements.sent-2411-115: In this setup, $\Yars_t \mid \bP_t$ follows a distribution that depends only on $\bP_t$, which we denote by $\mu_{1, \bP_t}$.
elements.sent-2411-116: Therefore, watermark detection is equivalent to the following hypothesis testing problem: \begin{equation}\label{eq:original-detectopn-problem} H_0: Y_t \sim \mu_0~~\text{i.i.d.~~for}~~1 \le t \le n \quad\text{versus}\quad H_1: Y_t\mid\bP_t \sim \mu_{1,\bP_t}~~\text{ for }~~1 \le t \le n.\end{equation}.

elements.paragraph-2411-006: The Gumbel-max watermark: Decoder and detection.
elements.sent-2411-117: Our paper focuses on the Gumbel-max watermark, one of the most influential and the first unbiased watermark \citep{scott2023watermarking , which has been implemented internally at OpenAI \citep{openai2024understanding and serves as a baseline in many studies.}}
elements.sent-2411-118: This watermark utilizes the Gumbel-max trick, a sampling technique for multinomial distributions \citep{gumbel1948statistical, papandreou2011perturb, maddison2014sampling, jang2016categorical .}
elements.sent-2411-119: The trick ensures that $\arg\max_{\token \in \Voca P_\token^{-1 \log U_\token$ follows the distribution $\bP = (P_\token)_{\token \in \Voca}$, where $\xi = (U_\token)_{\token \in \Voca}$ consists of $|\Voca|$ i.i.d.. $U(0,1)$ random variables.}}
elements.sent-2411-120: Scott Aaronson proposed the following unbiased decoder \citep{scott2023watermarking : \begin{equation}\label{eq:it} \SMmax(\bP, \xi) := \arg\max_{\token \in \Voca} \frac{\log U_w}{P_w}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-121: The pivotal statistic is $\Yars_t = Y(\token_t, \xi_t) = U_{t, \token_t}$, where $\xi_t = (U_{t, \token )_{\token \in \Voca}$ represents the pseudorandom numbers.}
elements.sent-2411-122: The watermark is detected when the sum-based statistic $\Sars_n = \sum_{t=1 ^n \hars(\Yars_t)$, with $\hars(y) = -\log(1-y)$, exceeds a set threshold.}

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Truncated GoF detection method (\Algo)} \label{alg:detection} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State Input: Edited text $\token_{1:n}$, hash function $\AM$, secret key $\Key$, pivot statistic function $\Yars$, stability parameter $c_n^+$, and critical value $\delta$. \State For $t = 1, 2, \ldots, n$, compute the pseudorandomness number $\xi_t = \AM(\token_{(t-m):(t-1)}, \Key)$. \State For $t = 1, 2, \ldots, n$, compute the pivot statistic $\Yars_t = \Yars(\token_t, \xi_t)$. \State For $t=1,2,\ldots, n$, calculate the p-value as $\rp_t = 1- \Yars_t$. \State Sort the p-values in the ascending order $\rp_{(1)} < \rp_{(2)}<\ldots < \rp_{(n)}$ and set $\rp_{(n+1)}=1$. \State Define the function $K_s^+$ as in \eqref{eq:Ks+} and compute the test statistic by \begin{equation} \label{eq:equivalent-Sn} S_n^+(s) = \sup_{t: \rp_{(t+1)} \ge c_n^+} K_s^+ (t/n, \rp_{(t)}). \end{equation} \State Claim: Text $\token_{1:n}$ is partially LLM-generated if \eqref{eq:goodness-of-fit-test} holds; otherwise, it is human-written. \end{algorithmic}

elements.subsection-2411-006: Modeling the Process of Human Edits

elements.sent-2411-201: This procedure simplifies and standardizes how humans modify LLM outputs, but it is still fairly general.
elements.sent-2411-202: The only requirement is that the human edits tokens in an autoregressive manner, meaning they cannot change tokens that have already been edited.
elements.algorithmic-2411-001: [1] \State Input: The watermarked text $\ttoken_{1:\tn}$ generated by $\ttoken_t = \SM(\tbP_t, \txi_t)$ and $\tbP_t = \MM(\ttoken_{1:(t-1)})$. \State Initialize: $\token_{1:0} = \emptyset$ and $t = t_0 = 1$. \While{the edit is not complete} \State Try to determine $\token_t$ by inspecting the referenced token $\ttoken_{t_0}$. \If{the user approves $\ttoken_{t_0}$} \State No edit: Set $\token_t = \ttoken_{t_0}$ and update $(t, t_0) \gets (t+1, t_0+1)$. \ElsIf{the user prefers to generate $\token_t$ themselves} \State Generate $\token_t \sim \hbP_t$ where $\hbP_t$ depends on $\token_{1:(t-1)}$ and $\ttoken_{1:(t_0-1)}$. \State Substitution: Update $(t, t_0) \gets (t+1, t_0+1)$. \State Insertion: Update $(t, t_0) \gets (t+1, t_0)$. \ElsIf{the user searches for a better alternative in the watermarked text} \State Deletion: Update $(t, t_0) \gets (t, t_0+1)$. Note that $\token_t$ remains undetermined at this stage. \EndIf \EndWhile \State Return: The edited text $\token_{1:t}$.
elements.sent-2411-203: This procedure constructs an edited text \(\token_{1:n}\) by alternatively scanning each token in the original text \(\ttoken_{1:\tn}\) and deciding whether and how to edit it.
elements.sent-2411-204: Let \(t\) denote the target position in the edited text, and \(t_0\) represent the current position in the watermarked text being scanned.
elements.sent-2411-205: To model this process, we assume that, if a user wishes to replace or insert a new token, they generate it according to a multinomial distribution \(\hbP_t\), which depends on both the existing edited text \(\token_{1:(t-1)}\) and the watermarked subtext \(\ttoken_{1:(t_0-1)}\) up to the current step.
elements.sent-2411-206: At each iteration, the length of the edited text increases by either copying the watermarked token \(\ttoken_{t_0}\) to position \(t\) (without edits) or by generating a new token (for substitution or insertion).
elements.sent-2411-207: Once \(\token_t\) is determined, \(t\) always increments by one, extending the edited text.
elements.sent-2411-208: However, \(t_0\) remains unchanged in the case of insertion, allowing \(\ttoken_{t_0}\) to be revisited in the next iteration.
elements.sent-2411-209: For all other operations, \(t_0\) also increments by one, advancing to the next token in the watermarked text.

elements.sent-2411-392: Statistical power: To evaluate the statistical power of different detection methods, we set the theoretical Type I error at $\alpha = 0.01$ by tuning the critical values, either using central limit theorem predictions or Monte Carlo simulations.
elements.sent-2411-393: We then assess the actual Type I errors using unwatermarked texts and Type II errors using watermarked texts.
elements.sent-2411-394: Robustness to edits: Almost any editing method tends to weaken watermark signals.
elements.sent-2411-395: We focus on three representative types of edits \citep{kuditipudi2023robust : random edits (including substitution, insertion, and deletion), adversarial edits, and roundtrip translation.}
elements.sent-2411-396: In random edits, a random fraction of the watermarked tokens is replaced, inserted, or deleted, with substitutions and insertions involving tokens uniformly selected from the vocabulary \(\Voca\).
elements.sent-2411-397: Adversarial edits, by contrast, are more targeted, selectively modifying watermarked tokens to maximize the removal of watermark signals within a given edit budget.
elements.sent-2411-398: Finally, roundtrip translation involves translating the text from English to French and back to English using another language model.
elements.sent-2411-399: Random and adversarial edits enable systematic control over the level of edits, while roundtrip translation reflects a more realistic scenario likely to be encountered in practice.

elements.sent-2411-466: In this paper, we have developed an adaptive and robust method for detecting watermarks in text generated by LLMs but subsequently edited by humans, along with theoretical guarantees corroborated by numerical experiments.
elements.sent-2411-467: We start by proposing a procedure for modeling the human editing process, which prompts us to formulate this problem as sparse mixture detection.
elements.sent-2411-468: Our method, which we call the \Algo~test, is shown to achieve the optimal detection boundary in the regime of increasing edit level and vanishing regularity of NTP distributions under the framework of \cite{li2024statistical .}
elements.sent-2411-469: In contrast, we show that sum-based detection rules are provably suboptimal in the sense that the detection region is strictly smaller than that of the \Algo~test.
elements.sent-2411-470: Additionally, we show that the \Algo~test continues to provide high detection efficiency when the edit level and regularity of NTP distributions remain constant.
elements.sent-2411-471: In contrast, sum-based detection rules fail to achieve robustness in either regime due to their inability to adapt to unknown specifics of problem instances.
elements.sent-2411-472: Our findings open new avenues for the robust detection of LLM watermarks.
elements.sent-2411-473: First, although we focus on the Gumbel-max watermark, which is perhaps the most influential, it would be valuable to investigate robust detection for other watermarking schemes \citep{xie2024debiasing , such as the inverse transform watermark \cite{kuditipudi2023robust and the green-red list watermark \cite{kirchenbauer2023watermark .}}}
elements.sent-2411-474: In these cases, the truncation technique underlying \Algo~could offer useful insights.
elements.sent-2411-475: More broadly, future research might consider alternative nonparametric testing approaches \citep{nikitin1995asymptotic,d2017goodness , such as the Kolmogorov--Smirnov test, Pearson's Chi-square test, and the Khmaladze--Aki statistic \citep{khmaladze1982martingale, podkorytova1994large , which may offer adaptivity comparable to or even surpassing goodness-of-fit tests in certain parameter regimes.}}
elements.sent-2411-476: Empirical evaluation of these classical nonparametric methods could motivate the development of new procedures tailored to specific challenges, such as high-temperature regimes, where \Algo~may undergo a decline in detection power.
elements.sent-2411-477: Additionally, detection power could be enhanced by incorporating spatial information from watermark signals, as human edits often form clusters within text.
elements.sent-2411-478: From a theoretical standpoint, it is noteworthy that the optimal detection boundary $q+2p=1$ is achieved through pivotal statistics, suggesting that investigating an information-theoretic detection boundary could yield interesting insights.
elements.sent-2411-479: Lastly, for interpretive purposes, estimating the watermark fraction may offer insights into the degree of human contribution in content generated collaboratively by LLMs and humans.

elements.subsection-2411-020: Higher Criticism and Its Optimal Adaptivity
elements.sent-2411-607: We first introduce Higher Criticism and then show that it achieves optimal adaptivity introduced in Section \ref{sec:optimality .}
elements.sent-2411-608: In literature, Higher Criticism (HC), as a non-parametric procedure, has shown success in the sparse detection problem \citep{donoho2004higher,tony2011optimal,cai2014optimal .}
elements.sent-2411-609: It is an instance of the above \Algo~test when $s=2$.
elements.sent-2411-610: We introduce it in more detail in the following.
elements.sent-2411-611: Given the observed pivotal statistics $\{\Yars_t\}_{t=1 ^n$, HC contains three steps:.}

elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{Binary search to compute the edit tolerance limit} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State Input: A watermarked sentence $\ttoken_{1:\tn}$ and a specified edit. \State Initial: Set $l = 1$, $u = \tn$, and generate a random permutation $\pi_0$ over the vocabulary $\Voca$. \While{$u - l \ge 2$} \State Compute the middle point $m = \left\lfloor\frac{u+l}{2}\right\rfloor$. \State Apply the considered edit type to corrupt the watermarked tokens $\ttoken_{\pi(1)},\ldots, \ttoken_{\pi(m)}$. \State Denote the resulting edited text by $\token_{1:n_1}^{(m)}$. \State Pass the first $n$ tokens of $\token_{1:n_1}^{(m)}$ to the detection method. \If{the detection method rejects the null hypothesis $H_0$} \State Set $l \gets m$ \Else \State Set $u \gets m$ \EndIf \EndWhile \State Output: The edit tolerance limit is $\frac{m}{\tn} \times 100\%$. \end{algorithmic} \label{algo:binary}

elements.sent-2411-940: This protocol unfolds over three stages: first, watermark embedding, where the model encodes a hidden signal into the generated text; second, human editing, where the user may modify the output; and finally, watermark detection, where the verifier analyzes the submitted text to detect any remaining watermark signal.
elements.sent-2411-941: We begin by describing the embedding mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-942: LLMs, such as GPT models \citep{radford2019language,brown2020language , generate text by sampling tokens autoregressively.}
elements.sent-2411-943: At each position $t$, given the previously generated tokens $\token_{1:(t-1) := \token_1 \token_2 \cdots \token_{t-1}$, the model computes a next-token prediction (NTP) distribution $\bP_t = (P_{t,w )_{w \in \Voca}$ and samples the next token $\token_t$ accordingly.}}
elements.sent-2411-944: To embed a watermark, this sampling process is modified in a deterministic yet secret-key-dependent way.
elements.sent-2411-945: Specifically, at each step $t$, the model computes a pseudorandom variable $\xi_t = \AM(\token_{(t-m):(t-1) , \Key)$, where $\AM$ is a cryptographic hash function, $\Key$ is the watermarking key, and $m$ is the context window size.}
elements.sent-2411-946: The token is then deterministically selected as $\token_t = \SM(\bP_t, \xi_t)$ using a decoding function $\SM$.
elements.sent-2411-947: The variable $\xi_t$ is termed pseudorandom because it behaves like a random variable, yet can be deterministically reconstructed by the verifier from the observed tokens $\token_{1:n}$, the key $\Key$, and the hash function $\AM$.

elements.sent-2411-955: In contrast, if the text is fully generated by a watermarked LLM, each token $\token_t$ is deterministically chosen based on $\zeta_t$ through the decoder function $\SM$.
elements.sent-2411-956: This observation reduces watermark detection to a problem of testing for statistical dependence between the observed tokens and the pseudorandom variables.

elements.sent-2411-958: The central tool in this framework is a pivotal statistic $\Yars_t = Y(\token_t, \zeta_t)$, where the function $Y(\cdot, \cdot)$ is designed so that the distribution of $\Yars_t$ is known whenever $\token_t$ and $\zeta_t$ are independent—regardless of the marginal distribution of $\token_t$.
elements.sent-2411-959: In this way, under the null hypothesis $H_0$, where the text is entirely human-written, the statistics $\Yars_t$ are i.i.d.
elements.sent-2411-960: Under the alternative hypothesis $H_1$, where the text is fully generated by a watermarked LLM, each $\Yars_t = Y(\SM(\bP_t, \zeta_t), \zeta_t) \mid \bP_t$ follows a distribution that depends on the NTP distribution $\bP_t$, denoted by $\mu_{1, \bP_t}$.
